Detailed Description: [Essential] Create a new session, clearing all loaded tracks, data, and groups, keeping only default genome annotations.
[Basic] Load a reference genome. Supports standard abbreviations (e.g. hg38) or local .genome/FASTA file paths.
[Core] Load BAM/VCF/BigWig etc. data. Supports local paths or remote URLs, with optional index file.
Save the current session state as a complete XML session file (absolute path recommended).
[Core Jump] Navigate to a specified locus. Supports gene names (e.g. MYC) or coordinates (e.g. chr1:1000-2000), multi-locus split view.
Define a Region of Interest (ROI) as a colored vertical highlight on the coordinate axis.
[Most Common] Sort by base at the view center (or specified position), clustering reads containing mutations (SNP/SNV).
[Common] Sort by the start position of read alignments.
[Common] Sort by the strand of read alignment. Useful for strand-specific sequencing data.
[SV Preferred] Sort by insert size of paired-end reads. Helps locate large deletions or insertions (structural variants).
Sort by sample name when multiple samples are loaded in a track.
Sort reads by Read Group (RG tag) defined in the BAM header.
Sort by the chromosome of the mate read. Excellent for detecting inter-chromosomal translocations.
For paired-end data, sort by the strand of Read 1.
Sort by base quality at the center position. Helps identify false positives from low-quality sequencing.
Sort by a user-defined BAM tag (e.g. HP, NM).
[Common] Group reads by base type at the center position (separates A/T/C/G into different blocks).
[Common] Group reads by strand.
[SV Preferred] Group by paired-end orientation (FR/RF/LL/RR). Helps discover inversions or tandem duplications.
Group by sample attribute.
Group by Read Group (RG tag).
Group by a specified BAM tag (e.g. CB tag commonly used in single-cell sequencing).
Group by the strand of Read 1 in paired-end data.
Group by the chromosome of the mate read.
Cancel all grouping operations, restoring the default continuous stacking.
[Common] Color by alignment strand (typically red for forward, blue for reverse).
[Common] Color by insert size and anomaly status, visually exposing structural variant sites.
Color reads by paired-end orientation. Useful for visualizing inversions, translocations, etc.
Auto-assign colors based on a specific tag's value range.
Assign distinct colors for different Read Groups.
Clear all coloring rules, restore default gray.
[Detailed View] Default mode. Each read shown on a separate line with full alignment details.
[High Coverage] Squish view: reduce row height to display as many reads as possible, suitable for high-depth regions.
[Coverage Overview] Collapse view: compress track height to minimum, hiding overlapping read details. For rough whole-genome coverage view.
[Paired View] Pair connection mode: draw thin gray lines connecting mate reads.
[Essential for Epigenetics/Quantitative] Fix the Y-axis data range for chart-type tracks, ensuring consistent comparison across samples.
Overlay multiple numeric tracks into a single view. Greatly facilitates ChIP-seq peak comparison.
Change the color of a specified track using RGB values (e.g. 255,0,0).
Set an alternate color for a specified track. Useful for negative values or separating strands.
Switch chart data tracks (e.g. BigWig) to logarithmic scale display.
[Batch Processing Essential] Set the absolute local folder path for saving subsequent snapshot images. e.g. C:/download/
[Core Output] Take a screenshot of the current IGV view. Supports .png or vector .svg format. e.g. screenshot_20260630.png
[Anti-Truncation] Set the maximum vertical pixel height for screenshots. Increase for tall captures; -1 for visible area only.
[Anti-Error] Set the sleep interval between batch commands to prevent network/I/O delays from causing unloaded data on capture.
Temporarily modify advanced low-level preference settings; reset after IGV restart.
Debug use in port mode: echo the input parameter to verify connectivity.
[Automation Cleanup] Fully exit and close the IGV process, releasing memory.
